core.text:
📊 Why Trump Won

Donald Trump has secured not only an overwhelming Electoral College win but also the popular vote—marking the first Republican popular vote victory since George W Bush in 2004. Whilst polls had given Trump a roughly 50% chance of winning (making him a slight betting favourite), the magnitude of his victory has surprised almost everyone.

Let's analyse the two major factors behind the win:

1️⃣ UNPRECEDENTED MINORITY VOTER PERFORMANCE
The data show a seismic shift in minority voting patterns:
• Black Voters: In Wisconsin, Trump secured 20% of the Black vote, up dramatically from 8% in 2020
• Hispanic Voters: In Pennsylvania, Trump won 40% of the Hispanic vote, building on his already unprecedented 30% in 2020
• Overall Impact: NBC projects Trump winning approximately one-third of all minority voters nationwide—potentially the best Republican performance ever (the previous ceiling was 25% pre-1964)

Why This Shift Happened:
• Different ethnic groups had specific motivations:
 - Arab Americans: Strong disapproval of Biden's Gaza policy
 - Young Black voters: Decreased Democratic Party loyalty compared to older generations
 - Hispanic voters: Growing alignment with conservative social values

• The Ideology Gap:
 - Minorities typically hold more socially conservative views than their voting patterns suggest
 - White Democrats have become increasingly progressive whilst minority voters remain moderate
 - Minorities are less likely to agree with left-coded statements about systemic racism
 - Strong support among minorities for border security and American exceptionalism

• Breaking the Taboo:
 - According to John B Murdoch (Financial Times), there's been a decline in the taboo against minorities voting Republican
 - This has allowed conservative minorities to align their votes with their ideology

2️⃣ THE ECONOMIC FACTOR
Despite Biden's objectively strong economic record:
• Lower inflation than peer economies
• Superior GDP growth compared to other large economies
• Successfully achieved "soft landing"
• Effective industrial policies (Inflation Reduction Act, CHIPS Act)

Voters remained unconvinced for several reasons:

• The Anti-Core Inflation Effect:
 - Voters focus heavily on food and fuel prices (what Bloomberg's John Authors calls "anti-core inflation")
 - Day-to-day price increases matter more than macro-economic indicators
 - These items have seen dramatic increases globally

• The Media Polarisation Effect:
 - Democrats and Republicans consume entirely different media
 - Unlike other countries (e.g., Germany) where economic perceptions generally align across political lines
 - Each side views the economy more favourably when their party is in power
 - This polarisation made it nearly impossible for Biden/Harris to convince Republicans of economic successes

Key point: Exit polls showed the economy as the second most important issue to voters (with "democracy" being first, likely among Harris voters). The combination of minority voter shifts and economic perceptions created a perfect storm for Trump's victory.

The result: A historic realignment of voting patterns and a clear message about the disconnect between macroeconomic indicators and voter sentiment. This election may mark a fundamental shift in American electoral politics.

How the democrats fumbled their relationship with one of the world's most influential people, and learnt the hard way that you don't bet against Elon Musk:

Pre-2020:
- Musk was a consistent donor to Democratic candidates throughout this period.
- He actively backed Hillary Clinton in the 2016 presidential race.
- Through Tesla and SpaceX, he positioned his companies to advance Democratic climate and innovation goals.
- At this time, he described himself as "half Democrat, half Republican".
- He maintained close collaboration with the Obama/Biden administration on clean energy initiatives.

2020
- In early 2020, Musk still publicly supported Biden's presidential campaign.
- On 9th May, he challenged California's COVID restrictions, threatening to move Tesla's operations.
- On 10th May, Democratic Assemblywoman Gonzalez tweeted "F*ck Elon Musk" in response.
- Despite these growing tensions, Musk still cast his vote for Biden in the presidential election.

2021
- Musk began January expressing optimism about the Biden presidency.
- In August, the White House excluded Tesla from its EV Summit, despite the company holding 74% of the US electric vehicle market.
- The administration ignored SpaceX's achievement of the first civilian spaceflight in September.
- Late 2021 saw growing friction over Biden's pro-union stance.
- Throughout this period, Tesla continued leading the EV transition despite institutional exclusion.

2022
- The early months saw Musk becoming increasingly critical of Democratic policies.
- In May, he publicly labelled the Democrats the "party of division & hate".
- He announced he would cast his first Republican vote.
- By October, Musk confirmed that Gonzalez's tweet had influenced Tesla's move to Texas.
- His last Democratic donation (to Sen. Coons) was now well in the past.

2023-24
- The Democrats launched attack ads targeting Musk through the Future Forward USA PAC.
- Party messaging began framing him as a "Bond villain".
- Vice President Harris led an intensifying criticism campaign.
- The party's focus shifted to attacking his wealth and tax contributions.
- Musk emerged as a major Republican ally and donor.

Lessons (which will likely be ignored):
1. The personal attack from Gonzalez's tweet had lasting repercussions for party relations.
2. The institutional snub of the EV Summit alienated America's leading electric vehicle innovator.
3. The party consistently chose escalation over engagement.
4. Democratic climate goals ultimately suffered from losing their most influential EV advocate.

The Democrats' choice to antagonise rather than engage constructively was definitely one of their worst mistakes of the last decade, and it contributed significantly to their loss today.
